our_argument_summary: Our review says Meyl's scalar wave Faraday cage penetration is 'Not Demonstrated' because it was published only in non-indexed journals, replication attempts explain results via classical near-field coupling, and CUORE experiment shows expected Faraday attenuation (20 dB at 10 Hz).

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline exists for this WIN. In inject_ai_layer.py, WIN-015 appears as a bare metadata entry: {id: WIN-015, title: 'Meyl scalar wave Faraday penetration', data_source: 'Meyl', year: 2000, status: 'confirmed'} with no parameters, formulas, observed values, or testable conditions. Notably, monitor.py domain 8 ('SAA Decay Rate TTB') contains a source field referencing 'WIN-015' but this is about SAA decay at Tsumeb — a completely different topic, suggesting a copy-paste numbering error in the dome's own code.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Everything is hardcoded. The WIN entry is a static metadata record with status='confirmed' but no computation, measurement, or validation logic. There is no data fetching, no tolerance check, no comparison to any measurement. This is a literature citation presented as a 'confirmed prediction.'
data_sources_used: None — references 'Meyl' as sole data source with year 2000
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None needed — the claim is asserted without any computational validation, so there are no tolerances or adaptive passes to apply.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review doesn't specifically discuss the dome's code for WIN-015, which is reasonable since there is essentially no code to discuss. However, the monitor.py domain 8 mislabeling is an interesting detail we could note — the dome's own audit engine references WIN-015 in a completely unrelated SAA decay context, suggesting the WIN numbering is not consistent across the codebase.

holes_found.description: CUORE citation is tangential and potentially misleading. CUORE is a neutrinoless double-beta-decay experiment at Gran Sasso, not a Faraday shielding study. Its Faraday room is incidental infrastructure. Citing CUORE as evidence that 'Faraday cages work' does not directly address Meyl's specific claim that his experimental apparatus showed signal transmission through a Faraday cage. A dome defender could argue: 'CUORE proves Faraday cages work in general — we never disputed that. We claim that scalar waves specifically penetrate them, and CUORE never tested for scalar waves.' The CUORE citation proves something nobody disputes (conventional EM shielding works) rather than addressing the specific claim.
holes_found.recommendation: Either drop the CUORE citation or reframe it: 'Even the most sensitive physics experiments (CUORE, KATRIN) rely on Faraday shielding and observe no anomalous penetration at any frequency — if scalar waves existed and penetrated shielding, these experiments would be fundamentally compromised.' This makes the argument indirect-but-stronger rather than non-sequitur.

holes_found.description: Our claim of '20 dB at 10 Hz' needs verification. The CUORE paper (arxiv 1710.05614) abstract reports 'factor 15 at 50 Hz' and 'factor 1000 above 1 kHz.' The 10 Hz figure appears to come from the full paper text (not abstract), where shielding is described as 'just below a factor 10 (20 dB) at 10 Hz.' While this appears to be a real measurement, it's from the full text, not the abstract — so it's harder for a reader to quickly verify. More importantly, 20 dB (factor 10) at 10 Hz is actually modest shielding, and Meyl's experiments operate at MHz frequencies (where CUORE shows 60-80 dB). By citing the weakest frequency, we're actually underselling our own argument.
holes_found.recommendation: If retaining CUORE, cite the relevant frequency range for Meyl's experiments (which use MHz resonant circuits). At those frequencies, CUORE shows >60 dB attenuation. The 10 Hz number, while accurate, is the worst case and irrelevant to Meyl's setup.

holes_found.description: Our rebuttal is thin. Three sentences for a claim that a dome defender could argue is one of the most physically interesting WINs. The near-field coupling explanation is mentioned but not developed. We don't explain WHY Meyl's experiment appeared to work, which leaves the door open for 'you can't explain it either.'
holes_found.recommendation: Expand the detail_evidence to briefly explain the near-field coupling mechanism: at short range (< λ/2π), evanescent fields from the transmitter coil can couple through seams, cable feedthroughs, or the finite conductivity of the cage itself. This is standard antenna near-field theory and fully explains Meyl's observations without invoking new physics. Cite Bruhn (2006) from TU Darmstadt for the mathematical refutation.

stronger_arguments.description: Meyl's scalar wave theory has been mathematically demolished by Gerhard W. Bruhn (TU Darmstadt). Bruhn showed that (1) Meyl misapplies Pohl's textbook equations, which describe transverse EM waves, not longitudinal ones; (2) Meyl writes field dependencies as E=E(r(t)) instead of E=E(r,t), a fundamental error in partial differential equations; (3) Meyl's equations violate the superposition principle; (4) Maxwell's equations do not admit time-dependent scalar wave solutions of the Meyl type. This is not 'some criticism' — it's a complete mathematical proof that the theoretical foundation is invalid.
sources: Bruhn, G.W., 'Comments on Meyl's Annual 2006', TU Darmstadt (https://www2.mathematik.tu-darmstadt.de/~bruhn/MeylAnual2006.html)
Kuhlke, D., Journal of Scientific Exploration, Vol. 22 No. 2, 2010

stronger_arguments.description: Meyl had a paper retracted. Retraction Watch (2013) reported on a paper by Meyl that was retracted from DNA and Cell Biology — the paper claimed to merge physics and biology using scalar wave theory. This is relevant context showing the broader scientific community's assessment of Meyl's credibility.

The dome model's code has NO validation for this WIN — it's purely a literature citation with status='confirmed'. This makes it one of the weakest WINs in the entire model. Unlike magnetic field measurements or Schumann resonance values where the dome's monitor.py at least pretends to validate against data, WIN-015 is just: 'Meyl said so, therefore confirmed.' The dome model treats a single unreplicated experiment from a fringe researcher as equivalent to INTERMAGNET magnetometer readings.

kernel_of_truth: Near-field electromagnetic coupling is a real and important phenomenon. In the reactive near-field zone (distance < λ/2π from the source), the electromagnetic field has significant longitudinal components and does not behave like a plane wave. Energy can couple between antennas in this zone via evanescent modes that attenuate differently than propagating waves. Faraday cages ARE less effective against near-field sources (especially magnetic near-fields, since a Faraday cage primarily shields electric fields unless it uses mu-metal). The kernel of truth is that at close range, electromagnetic coupling can appear to 'penetrate' a Faraday cage — but this is completely explained by standard near-field antenna theory and does not require 'scalar waves' as a new physical phenomenon. Meyl correctly identified a real observation (signal coupling through/around a cage at close range) but incorrectly attributed it to a fictitious mechanism.
deeper_analysis:
WIN-015 is one of the dome model's weakest claims, but our rebuttal is also one of our weakest — creating an odd situation where a bad claim gets an inadequate response. The core issue is that our review treats this as primarily an empirical question ('does a Faraday cage work?') when the real problems are theoretical and methodological.

Theoretically, Meyl's scalar wave framework has been demolished. Bruhn (TU Darmstadt, 2006) showed that Meyl's derivation contains fundamental mathematical errors: he misapplies Pohl's transverse wave equations as longitudinal ones, uses an incorrect functional dependency E=E(r(t)) instead of E=E(r,t), and produces equations that violate the superposition principle. Kuhlke (Journal of Scientific Exploration, 2010) independently confirmed that Meyl's 'dual field approach' actually describes plane transverse electromagnetic waves, not longitudinal ones, and that his 'Fundamental Field Equation' has no solutions demonstrating longitudinal electric waves. The theoretical foundation for scalar waves simply does not exist within Maxwell's electrodynamics.

Methodologically, Meyl's Faraday cage experiment has a straightforward classical explanation. His apparatus operates with resonant coils at close range — well within the reactive near-field zone. In this regime, evanescent fields can couple through cage seams, cable feedthroughs, ground loops, or the finite skin depth of the cage material. This is standard antenna near-field theory (see Jackson, Classical Electrodynamics, Section 9.1 on near-field zone behavior). The fact that Meyl observed 'electric' rather than 'magnetic' coupling further supports this — capacitive coupling through small apertures and seams is exactly what you'd expect from a leaky Faraday cage at close range. No new physics is required.

Our CUORE citation, while factually accurate (the paper does report ~20 dB at 10 Hz and >60 dB above 1 kHz), is tangential. CUORE is a neutrino experiment whose Faraday room is incidental infrastructure, not a test of scalar wave theory. A sharper argument would note that if 'scalar waves' could penetrate Faraday shielding, every precision physics experiment in the world — from CUORE to LIGO to KATRIN — would show unexplained noise. They don't. This is an absence-of-evidence argument, but given the sensitivity of these instruments (measuring at the attonewton scale), it's a powerful one.

The dome model's own code reveals how thin this WIN is. In inject_ai_layer.py, WIN-015 is a bare metadata entry with no parameters, no formula, and no validation — just 'Meyl, 2000, confirmed.' The monitor.py file doesn't even have a domain that tests this claim; domain 8 references 'WIN-015' but is actually about SAA decay rate at Tsumeb (a copy-paste error). This WIN is not a 'prediction' of the dome model in any meaningful sense — it's a citation of a contested experiment that the dome model claims as supporting evidence without any attempt to integrate it into the dome's own physics.

Recommendation: Keep 'Not Demonstrated' verdict but strengthen the evidence paragraph. Add Bruhn's mathematical refutation, explain the near-field coupling mechanism, and either reframe or drop the CUORE citation in favor of the broader argument that precision physics experiments worldwide depend on Faraday shielding working as Maxwell predicts.
